creator: vd@FreeBSD.org
text:
On 6 March 2014 15:11, Alexey Dokuchaev <danfe@freebsd.org> wrote:
> On Thu, Mar 06, 2014 at 02:43:00PM +0200, Vasil Dimov wrote:
>> --- /usr/ports/Mk/bsd.port.mk.orig    2014-03-06 14:38:26.000000000 +0200
>> +++ /usr/ports/Mk/bsd.port.mk    2014-03-06 14:38:15.000000000 +0200
>> @@ -5553,7 +5553,7 @@
>>  .endif
>>  .endfor
>>  .endif
>> -    @if [ -f ${PLIST} ]; then \
>> +    @if [ -f "${PLIST}" ]; then \
>>          ${SED} ${PLIST_SUB:S/$/!g/:S/^/ -e s!%%/:S/=/%%!/} ${PLIST}
>> >> ${TMPPLIST}; \
>>      fi
>
> There are, unfortunately, more than one case of blind trust that externally
> coming variable values are non-empty.  Quick grep for e.g. 'if \[' | grep
> -v \" can give you the picture.  Ideally, all of them should be reviewed,
> and if quotes are indeed missing (unless it's guaranteed that variable in
> question cannot be empty), then they should be added.
[...]

I will take this as an "won't be fixed in bsd.port.mk" answer. I have
added a quotes in portsearch's own Makefile (which contains a small
snippet from bsd.port.mk's generate-plist target for performance
reasons).

Btw, I do not think that "there are other problems like this" is
enough justification to not fix a given problem. In this case it is
obvious that if PLIST is empty then "if [ -f ]" will evaluate to true
and then "sed -e '...' " will just hang waiting for input on stdin.

Notice that in this case a problem induced by a single port spreads to
and affects a ports-tree wise operations.
